title: Mobile UX Visual Review
description: Codex visually inspects Paradisio Board mobile screenshots and critiques the design

conversation.message:
I need you to visually inspect mobile screenshots of the Paradisio Board app and give detailed UX critique.

The screenshots are at: docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/

There are 16 screenshots across 4 viewport sizes (iPhone SE 375x667, iPhone 12 390x844, Pixel 5 393x851, iPhone Pro Max 430x932) showing:
- Homepage default state (search bar, filters, result cards)
- Homepage with search query
- Homepage with WhatsApp + restaurant filters applied
- Business detail page (with sticky bottom CTA bar, badges, scores, map, description)

For each view, I need you to:
1. Use view_image to inspect each screenshot
2. Critique the mobile UX: readability, tap targets, spacing, information hierarchy, what works at each size, what breaks
3. Identify the top 3 things to fix
4. Suggest specific CSS or HTML changes
5. Note any differences between the 4 viewport sizes

Start by reading the current CSS and JS files for context, then inspect the screenshots.

I applied your top 3 fixes. The changes:

1. Filters are now a 2-column grid on mobile (single column on iPhone SE width)
2. Active filter chips with X to dismiss and a Clear All button
3. Result names are clamped to 2 lines
4. Scores use compact labels (Contact, Visible, Complete)
5. Sticky CTA bar has safe-area padding, content has env(safe-area-inset-bottom)
6. Generated timestamp hidden on mobile
7. Extra-small screen breakpoint for iPhone SE

New screenshots are at the same path: docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/

Inspect the new screenshots and tell me:
1. Did the fixes land well?
2. What's still off?
3. Is there a round 3 of polish needed, or is this good enough to ship?

I applied your top 3 fixes from the previous review. New screenshots at docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/ (just recaptured). Inspect them and tell me: did the fixes land well, what's still off, and is this shippable?

Round 2 mobile screenshot review complete. I re-read `paradisio_app/static/styles.css` and `paradisio_app/static/app.js`, then inspected the recaptured screenshots under `docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/`. Because the full-page list screenshots are extremely tall, I also generated first-viewport crops in `CODEX_ENDPOINT/tasks/ca82e984/artifacts/round2_crops/` for accurate visual comparison.

Short answer: yes, the fixes landed well. This is shippable for a first mobile version, with one important caveat: the list still renders hundreds of cards in broad states, producing enormous full-page screenshots and likely avoidable mobile DOM cost. That does not block visual shipment, but it should be the next product/engineering fix after this polish pass.

What improved:

1. Mobile filters are much better on 390px+ widths.
On iPhone 12, Pixel 5, and Pro Max, the 2-column filter grid makes the control area feel intentional instead of like a long form. The first result now appears comfortably in the first viewport, especially on the taller devices. The search field still has strong affordance and the selects remain large enough to tap.

2. Active filter chips are a clear win.
The WhatsApp-only state now visibly says `Has WhatsApp x`, and the restaurant + WhatsApp state shows both chips plus `Clear all`. That fixes the previous problem where selected filters were buried inside select fields. The chip color and spacing are readable and do not feel heavy.

3. Result cards scan better.
The 2-line name clamp, compact score labels, and tighter badge sizing make cards less noisy. The primary contact link is easier to find, and the first card on 390px+ devices now shows name, metadata, badges, scores, and CTA without feeling as tall as before.

4. Generated timestamp is gone from mobile.
This helped the top of the page. The header now feels more user-facing and less like an internal audit view.

5. Detail page spacing is mostly fixed.
The sticky CTA no longer visually collides with the main content in the first viewport. The map, score bars, and description have enough room. The sticky bar buttons are large and readable; `Message on WhatsApp` + `Call` + `IG` is a practical mobile action set.

What is still off:

1. iPhone SE is still vertically expensive.
At 375px, the CSS switches to one-column filters, so the old problem remains on the smallest viewport: search plus four selects push the first result very low. It is usable, but not elegant. If SE support matters, keep two columns at 375px too, or replace the four selects with a compact `Filters` disclosure on extra-small screens.

Suggested adjustment:
```css
@media (max-width: 380px) {
  .filters { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 7px; }
  .filter-select { font-size: 0.78rem; padding: 9px 8px; min-height: 42px; }
}
```
If select labels become cramped, a better version is one visible row with `Category`, `Area`, `Contact`, `Sort` short labels or a filter drawer.

2. Header/stats still consume a lot of first-screen space.
The stats are useful, but on mobile they compete with search. This is acceptable, but a round 3 polish could compress them into one muted line or hide secondary counts behind a small `Stats` details control.

3. Long names are visually controlled but still content-heavy.
Clamping works, but entries like `3 Bamboo Ecolodge - Cahuita, Limon, Costa Rica` still dominate because the dataset name repeats location that is already shown in metadata. A display-name cleanup would improve perceived quality more than more CSS.

4. Broad result states are still too large.
The default and WhatsApp screenshots are huge because hundreds of cards render. This remains the biggest non-visual issue. Add pagination, virtualization, or `Load more` before calling the list fully production-ready for mobile performance.

5. Detail page has mild action duplication.
Top buttons (`Call`, `Instagram`, `Google Maps`) plus sticky buttons are usable, but slightly redundant. I would leave it for now unless you want a tighter detail page. If polishing later, keep Google Maps near the map and let the sticky bar own WhatsApp/call/IG.

Viewport differences:

- iPhone SE 375x667: Usable but still the weakest. One-column filters make the page feel form-heavy, and the first result is delayed. The detail page is acceptable, but the sticky bar takes a meaningful share of the viewport.
- iPhone 12 390x844 and Pixel 5 393x851: Best balance. The 2-column filters land well, chips are clear, and the first card appears at a sensible point.
- iPhone Pro Max 430x932: Looks comfortable. The extra width helps names and chips; no obvious layout break. It still does not need a different design.

Ship recommendation:
Ship this round visually. The applied fixes solved the main UX issues from the first review. I would not block on another CSS polish pass unless iPhone SE is a hard target. The next meaningful work should be either:

1. Keep 2-column filters on 375px / make extra-small filters more compact.
2. Add `Load more` or virtualization for broad result sets.
3. Clean display names so location suffixes do not duplicate category/area metadata.

Artifacts generated:
`CODEX_ENDPOINT/tasks/ca82e984/artifacts/round2_crops/` contains top crops of the 20 inspected screenshots.

We've added a QR code system since your last review. Here's what changed:

1. New script: paradisio_app/generate_qr.py — generates a 300 DPI QR code PNG for every business, plus a redirect landing page and a print gallery at docs/paradisio_app/qr/index.html
2. Each business page now shows a QR preview section between the map and the scores — a small QR image, text inviting the owner to print it, and a download link
3. QR encodes a relative path (../businesses/{slug}.html) which works from any deployment
4. The QR print gallery is a grid of all 750 QR codes with print CSS

New screenshots are at docs/paradisio_app/screenshots/mobile/ — the business pages now include the QR section near the bottom.

Also, the QR gallery page is at docs/paradisio_app/qr/index.html — it has a responsive grid layout with print styles.

Please inspect:
1. The QR section on the business detail page in mobile view — does it integrate well?
2. The QR gallery page — is the print layout usable?
3. Is there anything missing from the QR flow?
4. What's the overall state of the app now — ship or iterate?
